prompt: We split our monolith into 8 microservices six months ago. Every feature still requires deploying 3-4 services together. Is this normal for microservices?
expected: Skill activates and diagnoses a distributed monolith: coordinated multi-service deployment is the deployment-coupling indicator that the service boundaries are wrong because they were drawn by technical convenience rather than Bounded Contexts; it prescribes not merging services back but redrawing boundaries via Event Storming and migrating domain logic to the correct contexts.
notes: rationale: Describes the distributed monolith symptom (deployment coupling) — the skill's central diagnostic.; source id: tp-01

prompt: Our CTO says moving to microservices will fix our coupling problems. What questions should we ask before starting?
expected: Skill activates and surfaces the counterintuitive claim that microservices are infrastructure and do not automatically reduce logical coupling; the key questions to ask are what domain analysis (Event Storming / Bounded Context identification) will inform the boundaries, warning that splitting without it just reproduces the monolith's coupling across a network as a distributed monolith.
notes: rationale: Directly invites the counterintuitive claim: microservices don't fix coupling unless boundaries are right.; source id: tp-02

prompt: We're planning to split our Go monolith by team ownership — each team gets one service. What are the risks?
expected: Skill activates and identifies team-ownership splitting as one of the wrong criteria (naive Conway's Law) that preserves the original coupling; it warns this risks a distributed monolith and prescribes splitting along Bounded Context boundaries from Strategic DDD instead, with team structure following domain boundaries rather than defining them.
notes: rationale: Team-based splitting (Conway's Law applied naively) is one of the wrong splitting criteria the book contrasts with Bounded Context-based splitting.; source id: tp-03

prompt: Our microservices share a common 'models' Go module that all services import. A change to one model requires updating all services. How do we break this dependency?
expected: Skill activates and names the shared domain model distributed via a common module as a distributed-monolith indicator — internal model coupling across services; it prescribes redrawing Bounded Contexts so each service owns its own model and integrating across contexts via domain events rather than a shared struct module (composing with anti-dry-separate-read-write-models).
notes: rationale: Shared domain model across services is the distributed monolith indicator — internal model distributed via a shared module.; source id: tp-04

prompt: How do I configure service discovery for microservices in Kubernetes?
notes: rationale: Infrastructure/operations question — not about domain coupling or service boundary design.; source id: tp-05

prompt: What are the performance differences between gRPC and REST for inter-service communication in Go?
notes: rationale: Transport protocol comparison — not about coupling, bounded contexts, or why microservices fail.; source id: tp-06

prompt: We're considering serverless functions to replace our monolith. Will this improve our maintainability and reduce coupling?
expected: Skill applies the infrastructure-vs-domain distinction: serverless solves only infrastructure/deployment/scaling problems and does not stop you from building a hard-to-maintain, coupled application; the defensible answer is that maintainability and coupling depend on Bounded Context boundaries, not the runtime — moving to functions without domain analysis just yields a distributed monolith of functions.
notes: rationale: Serverless is an infrastructure choice the book explicitly calls out as not solving domain coupling — needs the distinction between infrastructure and domain decisions.; source id: tp-07

prompt: Should service boundaries align with team ownership (Conway's Law) or with domain boundaries? We're getting conflicting advice.
expected: Skill resolves the conflict in favor of domain boundaries: service lines should be drawn from Bounded Contexts identified via Strategic DDD, and team structure should follow those domain boundaries rather than define them; team-based splitting applied first is the naive-Conway trap that produces wrong boundaries and a distributed monolith.
notes: rationale: Conway's Law vs. Bounded Context — the book's position is that domain boundaries come from Strategic DDD, not team structure; team structure should follow domain boundaries, not define them.; source id: tp-08

prompt: We have 15 microservices but all 15 deployments require the same database migration to run. How did this happen and how do we fix it?
expected: Skill identifies a shared database as a distributed-monolith indicator: the services never achieved data ownership independence because boundaries were drawn without Bounded Context analysis; the fix is to redraw contexts so each owns its own data, migrating gradually (composing with anti-dry-separate-read-write-models) rather than merging services back.
notes: rationale: Shared database is a distributed monolith indicator — but the fix involves Bounded Context analysis (this skill) and possibly the anti-dry-separate-read-write-models skill.; source id: tp-09
